title: Thermodynamic Invariants of Coupled Channels: A Many-Channel Tolman-Ehrenfest Effect
authors: Benjamin Hamblin, Victor Calo, Klaus Regenauer-Lieb
abstract: When multiple thermodynamic channels are coupled, single-channel equilibrium conditions fail. Extending the Tolman--Ehrenfest effect to the entropy manifold, we derive the unique $n$-channel invariant $ζ_i T_i = C$, where $ζ_i$ is the holonomy of the Ruppeiner connection. For the granular volume--stress ensemble, Rowe's dilatancy ratio and energy restriction emerge as geometric consequences of the off-diagonal curvature $g_{Vσ}$, and the 60-year puzzle of state-dependent $K_μ$ is resolved: the correction $ζ_V$ reaches $O(1)$ near jamming. The prediction $ζ_Vχ=\mathrm{const}$ across a shear band is experimentally testable.
summary: This theoretical paper generalizes the Tolman-Ehrenfest effect to coupled thermodynamic channels, deriving a universal invariant $\zeta_i T_i = C$. It applies this geometric framework to granular materials, successfully resolving long-standing puzzles regarding state-dependent material constants. The work demonstrates how geometric invariants derived from the entropy manifold can predict macroscopic physical laws in complex, coupled systems.
main_problem: The paper addresses the failure of single-channel equilibrium conditions when multiple thermodynamic channels are coupled, aiming to derive a universal invariant condition.
main_result: The unique equilibrium invariant is found to be $\zeta_i T_i = C$, where $\zeta_i$ is the holonomy of the Ruppeiner connection, which resolves long-standing puzzles in granular physics.
method: The analysis extends the Tolman-Ehrenfest effect to the entropy manifold, utilizing the Ruppeiner metric and deriving the equilibrium condition as a first integral of the level-set flow.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to systems with $n$ coupled thermodynamic channels, with the canonical realization being the $N=2$ volume–stress granular ensemble.
key_observables: The invariant $\zeta_i T_i = C$, the off-diagonal curvature $g_{V\sigma}$, the dilatancy ratio, and the MCTE-corrected Rowe relation $\sigma'^{1/3} = \zeta_V(\sigma) K_\mu R$.
important_parameters: The coupling coefficient $\zeta_i$, the jamming point ($\phi_J$), and the dimensionality of the system ($N=2$ being the primary focus).
main_assumptions: The $N=2$ volume–stress ensemble is used as the canonical model, and the results are derived independently of the full $N=3$ non-associated flow dynamics.
figures_summary: Figure 1 contrasts classical decoupled vs. coupled (MCTE) behavior showing the invariant. Figure 2 illustrates the MCTE invariance on a toy entropy surface, showing the product $\zeta_1 T_1$ remains constant despite variations in bare compactivity.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the failure of single-channel equilibrium, derives the MCTE invariant $\zeta_i T_i = C$ using the Ruppeiner metric, and then applies this general formalism to predict and resolve specific issues in granular plasticity (dilatancy, energy restriction, $K_\mu$ dependence).
why_interesting: While focused on granular physics, the mathematical machinery—using geometric invariants (Ruppeiner connection, curvature) to define equilibrium in coupled systems—is highly relevant to understanding non-equilibrium thermodynamics in complex quantum many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Three-panel schematic of the MCTE framework (col- umn width). (a) Classical (gij = 0): entropy level sets are straight lines; each intensity Ti is independently uniform. (b) MCTE (gij ̸= 0): level sets curve; a closed loop accumu- lates a non-trivial Ruppeiner holonomy H ωi ̸= 0 (red), and the coupling coefficient ζV grows in the direction of increasing off-diagonal curvature (orange). The unique first integral of this flow is ζiTi = C. (c) ζV as a function of packing fraction ϕ: unity in the dilute limit, O(1) departure at jamming ϕJ, explaining the observed rise in Rowe’s Kµ.

FIG. 2. MCTE invariance on the toy entropy surface S = a ln(V −VJ) + b ln(σmax−σ) −cσ/(V −VJ) with c = 0.3, V0 = 0.78. (a) Bare compactivity χ varies by a factor of 3 along the entropy level set. (b) Coupling coefficient ζ1 compensates. (c) Product ζ1T1 is constant to 10−13 (machine precision), verifying the MCTE relation. Full parameter study in the Supplemental Material [13].
